Annual Exam - 2023

Code: 65/4/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  3. Please check that this question paper contains 38 questions.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five Sections - A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A, Questions no. 1 to 18 are multiple choice questions (MCQs) and questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B, Questions no. 21 to 25 are very short answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C, Questions no. 26 to 31 are short answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D, Questions no. 32 to 35 are long answer (LA) type questions carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E, Questions no. 36 to 38 are case study based questions carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section B, 3 questions in Section C, 2 questions in Section D and 2 questions in Section E.
  9. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION-A

This section comprises multiple choice questions (MCQs) of 1 mark each.
1.

If A is a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix and B is a matrix such that A'B and AB' are both defined, then the order of the matrix B is:

a. 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
b. 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
c. 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
d. 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
2.

If area of triangle is 35 sq units with vertices (2, -6), (5, 4) and (k, 4). Then k is

a. 12
b. -12, -2
c. 2, -2
d. -2
3.

If f(x) = 2|x| + 3 |sin x| + 6, then the right hand derivative of f(x) at x = 0 is:

a. 6
b. 5
c. 3
d. 2
4.

If {tex}\mathrm{x}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]+\mathrm{y}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\frac{4}{9}\right]{/tex}, then:

a. x = 1, y = 2
b. x = 2, y = 1
c. x = 1, y = -1
d. x = 3, y = 2
5.

If a matrix A = [1 2 3], then the matrix AA' (where A' is the transpose of A) is:

a. 14
b. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
c. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
d. [14]
6.

The product {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} a & b \\ -b & a \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} a & -b \\ b & a \end{array}\right]{/tex} is equal to:

a. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
b. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} (a+b)^2 & 0 \\ (a+b)^2 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
c. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 & 0 \\ \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
d. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{a} & 0 \\ 0 & \mathrm{~b} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
7.

Distance of the point (p, q, r) from y-axis is:

a. q
b. |q|
c. |q| + |r|
d. {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{p}^2+\mathrm{r}^2}{/tex}
8.

The solution set of the inequation 3x + 5y < 7 is:

a. whole xy-plane except the points lying on the line 3x + 5y = 7.
b. whole xy-plane along with the points lying on the line 3x + 5y = 7.
c. open half plane containing the origin except the points of line 3x + 5y = 7.
d. open half plane not containing the origin.
9.

If {tex}\int\limits_0^\text a{/tex} 3x2 dx = 8, then the value of a is:

a. 2
b. 4
c. 8
d. 10
10.

The sine of the angle between the vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} = {tex}3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\sqrt{\frac{5}{21}}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{5}{\sqrt{21}}{/tex}
c. {tex}\sqrt{\frac{3}{21}}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{4}{\sqrt{21}}{/tex}
11.

The order and degree (if defined) of the differential equation, {tex}\left(\frac{\text d^2 \text y}{\text {dx}^2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}\right)^3{/tex} = x sin {tex}\left(\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}\right){/tex}respectively are:

a. 2, 2
b. 1, 3
c. 2, 3
d. 2, degree not defined
12.

{tex}\int{/tex}e5 log x dx is equal to:

a. {tex}\frac{\text x^5}{5}+\text C{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{\text x^6}{6}+\text C{/tex}
c. 5x4 + C
d. 6x5 + C
13.

A unit vector along the vector {tex}4\hat {\text i} - 3\hat {\text k}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\frac{1}{7}(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{1}{5}(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}(4 \hat{\text i}-3 \hat{\text k}){/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}(4 \hat{\text i}-3 \hat{\text k}){/tex}
14.

Which of the following points satisfies both the inequations 2x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10 and x + 2y {tex}\geq{/tex} 8?

a. (-2, 4)
b. (3, 2)
c. (-5, 6)
d. (4, 2)
15.

If y = sin2 (x3), then {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} is equal to:

a. 2 sin x3 cos x3
b. 3x3 sin x3 cos x3
c. 6x2 sin x3 cos x3
d. 2x2 sin2 (x3)
16.

The point (x, y, 0) on the xy-plane divides the line segment joining the points (1, 2, 3) and (3, 2, 1) in the ratio:

a. 1 : 2 internally
b. 2 : 1 internally
c. 3 : 1 internally
d. 3 : 1 externally
17.

Two events E and F are independent. If P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 then P(E|F) - P(F|E) equals

a. {tex}\frac{1}{70}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{3}{35}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{1}{7}{/tex}
18.

The integrating factor for solving the differential equation {tex}\text x \frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} - y = 2x2 is:

a. e-y
b. e-x
c. x
d. {tex}\frac {1}{\text x}{/tex}

Questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion and Reason based questions carrying 1 mark each. Two statements are given, one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (a), (b), (c) and (d) as given below.
19.

Assertion (A): The lines {tex}\vec {\text r}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {\text r}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}+\mu \overrightarrow{b_2}{/tex} are perpendicular, when {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}_1} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}_2}{/tex} = 0.
Reason (R): The angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between the lines {tex}\vec {\text r}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {\text r}{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}+\mu \overrightarrow{b_2}{/tex} is given by cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\overrightarrow{b_1} \cdot \overrightarrow{b_2}}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\right|\left|\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|}{/tex}.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
20.

Assertion (A): All trigonometric functions have their inverses over their respective domains.
Reason (R): The inverse of tan-1 x exists for some x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\mathbb R{/tex}.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.

SECTION-B

This section comprises very short answer (VSA) type questions of 2 marks each.
21.

If xy = ex-y, then show that {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{\text y(\text x-1)}{\text x(\text y+1)}{/tex}.
22.(i)

Find the domain of y = sin-1 (x2 - 4).

OR
(ii)

Evaluate: {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left[\cos \left(-\frac{7 \pi}{3}\right)\right]{/tex}
23.

If the projection of the vector {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} on the vector {tex}p \hat{\text i}+\hat{\text j}-2 \hat{\text k}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}, then find the value(s) of p.
24.

Find the point on the curve y2 = 8x for which the abscissa and ordinate change at the same rate.
25.(i)

Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point (2, 1, 3) and perpendicular to both the lines {tex}\frac {\text x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac {\text y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac {\text z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex}; {tex}\frac {\text x } { - 3 } = \frac {\text y } { 2 } = \frac {\text z } { 5 }{/tex}.

OR
(ii)

The equations of a line are 5x - 3 = 15y + 7 = 3 - 10z. Write the direction cosines of the line and find the coordinates of a point through which it passes.

SECTION-C

This section comprises short answer (SA) type questions of 3 marks each.
26.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{2}{(1-\text x)\left(1+\text x^2\right)}\text {dx}{/tex}
27.(i)

Evaluate:  {tex}\int\limits_{\frac 13}^{1} \frac{\left(\text x-\text x^{3}\right)^{\frac 13}}{\text x^{4}} \text {dx}{/tex}

OR
(ii)

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_1^3{/tex}{|(x - 1)| + |(x - 2)|} dx
28.

Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically:
Maximize Z = 5x + 3y
subject to the constraints
3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15,
5x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10,
x, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
29.

From a lot of 30 bulbs which include 6 defective bulbs, a sample of 2 bulbs is drawn at random one by one with replacement. Find the probability distribution of the number of defective bulbs and hence find the mean number of defective bulbs.
30.(i)

Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}} = \frac{\text x+\text y}{\text x}{/tex}, y(1) = 0.

OR
(ii)

Find the general solution of the differential equation
ex tan y dx + (1 - ex)sec2y dy = 0.
31.(i)

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_{\frac \pi 4}^{\frac \pi 2} \text e^{2 \text x}\left(\frac{1-\sin 2 \text x}{1-\cos 2 \text x}\right) \text {dx}{/tex}

OR
(ii)

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits _ { - 2 } ^ { 2 } \frac {\text x ^ { 2 } } { 1 + 5 ^ {\text x } } \text {dx}{/tex}.

SECTION-D

This section comprises long answer (LA) type questions of 5 marks each.
32.(i)

Find the image of the point (2, -1, 5) in the line {tex}\frac{\text x-11}{10}=\frac{\text y+2}{-4}=\frac{\text z+8}{-11}{/tex}.

OR
(ii)

Vertices B and C of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}{/tex} lie on the line {tex}\frac{\text x+2}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text y-1}{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z}{4}{/tex}. Find the area of {tex}\triangle \text {ABC}{/tex} given that point A has coordinates (1, -1, 2) and the line segment BC has length of 5 units.
33.

Find the inverse of the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}. Using the inverse A-1, solve the system of linear equations
x - y + 2z = 1; 2y - 3z = 1; 3x - 2y + 4z = 3.
34.

Using integration, find the area of the region bounded by the parabola y2 = 4ax and its latus rectum.
35.(i)

If N denotes the set of all natural numbers and R be the relation on N {tex}\times{/tex} N defined by (a, b) R (c, d), if ad(b + c) = bc(a + d). Show that R is an equivalence relation.

OR
(ii)

Let f: {tex}\mathbb{R}-\left\{-\frac{4}{3}\right\} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}{/tex} be a function defined as f(x) = {tex}\frac{4 \text x}{3 \text x+4}{/tex}. Show that f is a one-one function. Also, check whether f is an onto function or not.

SECTION-E

This section comprises 3 case study based questions of 4 marks each.

Case Study-1
36.

A building contractor undertakes a job to construct 4 flats on a plot along with parking area. Due to strike the probability of many construction workers not being present for the job is 0.65. The probability that many are not present and still the work gets completed on time is 0.35. The probability that work will be completed on time when all workers are present is 0.80.
Let: E1: represent the event when many workers were not present for the job;
E2: represent the event when all workers were present; and
E: represent completing the construction work on time.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i)

What is the probability that all the workers are present for the job?

 1
(ii)

What is the probability that construction will be completed on time?

 1
(iii)(a)

What is the probability that many workers are not present given that the construction work is completed on time?

 2

OR
(iii)(b)

What is the probability that all workers were present given that the construction job was completed on time?

 2

Case Study-2
37.

Let f(x) be a real valued function. Then its

  • Left Hand Derivative (L.H.D.): Lf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a-\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{-\text h}{/tex}
  • Right Hand Derivative (R.H.D.): Rf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a+\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{\text h}{/tex}

Also, a function f(x) is said to be differentiable at x = a if its L.H.D. and R.H.D. at x = a exist and both are equal.
For the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} |\text x-3|, \text x \geq 1 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \text x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
(i)

What is R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1?

 1
(ii)

What is L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1?

 1
(iii)(a)

Check if the function f(x) is differentiable at x = 1.

 2

OR
(iii)(b)

Find f'(2) and f'(-1).

 2

Case Study-3
38.

Sooraj's father wants to construct a rectangular garden using a brick wall on one side of the garden and wire fencing for the other three sides as shown in the figure. He has 200 metres of fencing wire.

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i)

Let x metres denote the length of the side of the garden perpendicular to the brick wall and y metres denote the length of the side parallel to the brick wall. Determine the relation representing the total length of fencing wire and also write A(x), the area of the garden.

 2
(ii)

Determine the maximum value of A(x).

 2

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.

SECTION-A

1.

If A is a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix and B is a matrix such that A'B and AB' are both defined, then the order of the matrix B is:

c. 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
d. 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
Correct Answer: (a) 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
Explanation:

We have to find: Order of A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
Order of A’ = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
As ATB and BAT are both defined, so the number of columns in B should be equal to the number of rows in A’ for BA’ and also the number of columns in A’ should be equal to the number of rows in A’ for BA’.

Two events E and F are independent. If P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5 then P(E|F) - P(F|E) equals

Correct Answer: (a) {tex}\frac{1}{70}{/tex}
Explanation:

Here P(E) = 0.3, P(E {tex}\cup{/tex} F)=0.5
Let P(F) = x
{tex}\therefore P(E \cup F)=P(E)+P(F)-P(E \cap F){/tex}
{tex}=P(E)+P(F)-P(E) \cdot P(F){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 0.5 = 0.3 + x - 0.3x
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{0.5-0.3}{0.7}=\frac{2}{7}=P(F){/tex}
{tex}\therefore P(E / F)-P(F / E)=\frac{P(E \cap F)}{P(F)}-\frac{P(F \cap E)}{P(E)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F) \cdot P(E)-P(F \cap E) \cdot P(F)}{P(E) \cdot P(F)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{P(E \cap F)[P(E)-P(F)]}{P(E \cap F)}=P(E)-P(F){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{10}-\frac{2}{7}=\frac{21-20}{70}=\frac{1}{70}{/tex}

Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point (2, 1, 3) and perpendicular to both the lines {tex}\frac {\text x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac {\text y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac {\text z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex}; {tex}\frac {\text x } { - 3 } = \frac {\text y } { 2 } = \frac {\text z } { 5 }{/tex}.

Solution:

We have to find the vector and cartesian equations of the line passing through the point (2,1,3) and perpendicular to the lines {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex}and {tex}\frac { x } { - 3 } = \frac { y } { 2 } = \frac { z } { 5 }.{/tex}

We know that any line through the point (2, 1, 3) can be written as
{tex}\frac { x - 2 } { a } = \frac { y - 1 } { b } = \frac { z - 3 } { c }{/tex}........................(i)
where, a, b and c are the direction ratios of line (i).
{tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex}
and {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 0 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 0 } { 5 }{/tex}
Direction ratios of these two lines are (1,2,3) and (-3, 2,5), respectively.
We know that, if two lines are perpendicular,
then
{tex}a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad a + 2 b + 3 c = 0{/tex} ....................(ii)
and {tex}\quad - 3 a + 2 b + 5 c = 0{/tex} ................(iii)
In Equations. (ii) and (iii), by cross-multiplication,
we get
{tex}\frac { a } { 10 - 6 }=\frac { b } { - 9 - 5 } = \frac { c } { 2 + 6 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 4 } = \frac { b } { - 14 } = \frac { c } { 8 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { b } { - 7 } = \frac { c } { 4 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad a = 2 \lambda , b = - 7 \lambda{/tex}
and {tex}c = 4 \lambda{/tex}
On substituting the values of a, band c in Equation(i),
we get ,
{tex}\frac { x - 2 } { 2 \lambda } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 \lambda } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 }{/tex}
which is the required cartesian equation of the line.
The vector equation of line which passes through (2,1,3) and parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} is 
{tex}\vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ){/tex}
which is the required vector equation of the line.

26.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{2}{(1-\text x)\left(1+\text x^2\right)}\text {dx}{/tex}

Solution:

Let {tex}\frac{2}{(1-\text x)\left(1+\text x^2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text A}{1-\text x}+\frac{\text B \text x+\text C}{1+\text x^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = 1, B = 1, C = 1
Hence, I = {tex}\int \frac{2}{(1-\text x)\left(1-\text x^2\right)} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\int\left[\frac{1}{1-\text x}+\frac{\text x+1}{1+\text x^2}\right] \mathrm{dx}{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{1}{1-\text x} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{\text x+1}{\text x^2+1} \mathrm{dx}{/tex}
= {tex}\int \frac{1}{1-\text x} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2 \text x}{\text x^2+1} \mathrm{dx}{/tex} + {tex}\int \frac{1}{\text x^2+1} \mathrm{dx}{/tex}
= -log |1 - x| + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}log (x2 + 1) + tan-1(x) + C

Evaluate:  {tex}\int\limits_{\frac 13}^{1} \frac{\left(\text x-\text x^{3}\right)^{\frac 13}}{\text x^{4}} \text {dx}{/tex}

Solution:

Let {tex}I=\int\limits_{\frac 13}^{1} \frac{\left(\text x-\text x^{3}\right)^{\frac 1 3}}{\text x^{4}} \text {dx}{/tex} , then we have
{tex}I=\int\limits_{\frac{1}{3}}^{1} \frac{\left(\frac{1}{\text x^{2}}-1\right)^{\frac 13}}{\text x^{3}} \text {dx}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Let } \frac{1}{x^{2}}-1=t{/tex}  then {tex}\frac{-2}{x^{3}} d x=d t{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow t=8 \text { and } x=1 \Rightarrow t=0{/tex}
{tex}I=-\frac{1}{2} \int_{8}^{0}(t)^{\frac 13} d t{/tex}
{tex}I=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{t^{8}}{\frac 4 3}\right]_{8}^{0}{/tex}
{tex}I=-\frac{1}{2}[0-12]=6{/tex}

Solve the Linear Programming Problem graphically:
Maximize Z = 5x + 3y
subject to the constraints
3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15,

x, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.

Region represented by 3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15: 
The line meets coordinate axes at A2(5,0) and B2(0,3) respectively. Join these points to obtain the line 3x + 5y = 15. 
Clearly, (0,0) satisfies the inequation 3x + 5y {tex}\leq{/tex} 15, so, the region in xy -plane contains the origin represents the solution set of the given inequation.
The region represented by x {tex}\geq {/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq {/tex} 0: It clearly represents the first quadrant of xy plane.
Common region to regions represented by above inequalities is shown as shaded region. 
The coordinates of the corner points of the shaded region are O(0,0), A(2,0), P{tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex}, B2(0,3)
The value of Z = 5x + 3y at
O(0,0) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 0
A(2, 0) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 10
P{tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{20}{19}{/tex} + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{45}{19}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{235}{19}{/tex}
B2(0,3) = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 9
Clearly, z is maximum at P{tex}\left(\frac{20}{19}, \frac{45}{19}\right){/tex}
So, x = {tex}\frac{20}{19}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{45}{19}{/tex}, and maximum value of Z = {tex}\frac{235}{19}{/tex}

29.

From a lot of 30 bulbs which include 6 defective bulbs, a sample of 2 bulbs is drawn at random one by one with replacement. Find the probability distribution of the number of defective bulbs and hence find the mean number of defective bulbs.

Solution:

Let X be the random variable which denotes the number of defective bulbs drawn from a sample of 2 bulbs. Here X may take values 0, 1 or 2.
Let A and B be the event of drawing a defective bulb and non defective bulb respectively.
P(A) = {tex}\frac{6}{30}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}; P(B) = {tex}1-\frac{1}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
Now P(X = 0) = {tex}\frac{4}{5} \times \frac{4}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{16}{25}{/tex}
P(X = 1) = {tex}2\left[\frac{4}{5} \times \frac{1}{5}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8}{25}{/tex}
P(X = 2) = {tex}\frac{1}{5} \times \frac{1}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex}
Thus P.D. of X is

X 0 1 2
P(X) {tex}\frac {16}{25}{/tex} {tex}\frac {8}{25}{/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{25}{/tex}
XP(X) 0 {tex}\frac {8}{25}{/tex} {tex}\frac {2}{25}{/tex}

Mean = {tex}\Sigma{/tex} X P(X) = {tex}\frac{10}{25}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}

30.(i)

Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}} = \frac{\text x+\text y}{\text x}{/tex}, y(1) = 0.

Solution:

{tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text x+\text y}{\text x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 1 + {tex}\frac{\text y}{\text x}{/tex}
Let {tex}\frac{\text y}{\text x}{/tex} = v. Then x {tex}\frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} + v = {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}
So, Differential equation becomes.
{tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\text {dv}}{\text {dx}}+\mathrm{v}{/tex} = 1 + v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dv = {tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = log |x| + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x log |x| + cx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0, y = x log |x|
[can also be solved, taking first order linear diff. eqn]

OR

Find the general solution of the differential equation
ex tan y dx + (1 - ex)sec2y dy = 0.

Solution:

According to the question,The given differential equation is
ex tan y dx + (1 - ex)sec2y dy = 0
On separating the variables, we get
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex}
Therefore,on integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \frac { e ^ { x } } { e ^ { x } - 1 } d x = \int \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } y } { \tan y } d y{/tex}
Therefore,on putting ex - 1 = t and tan y = z, we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow e ^ { x } d x = d t \text { and } \sec ^ { 2 } y d y = d z{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \int \frac { d t } { t } = \int \frac { d z } { z }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log | t | = \log | z | + \log C \left[ \because \int \frac { 1 } { x } d x = \log | x | \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log \left| e ^ { x } - 1 \right| = \log | \tan y | + \log C{/tex} {tex}[ \because \log m + \log n = \log m n ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad e ^ { x } - 1 = C \tan y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \tan y = \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { e ^ { x } - 1 } { C } \right){/tex}
which is the required solution.

32.(i)

Find the image of the point (2, -1, 5) in the line {tex}\frac{\text x-11}{10}=\frac{\text y+2}{-4}=\frac{\text z+8}{-11}{/tex}.

Solution:


Let the given point be P(2, –1, 5)
Coordinates of M are ({tex}10\lambda{/tex} + 11, {tex}-4\lambda{/tex} – 2, {tex}-11\lambda{/tex} – 8) where M is the foot of perpendicular from P.
Direction ratios of PM are ({tex}10\lambda{/tex} + 9, {tex}-4\lambda{/tex} – 1, {tex}-11\lambda{/tex} – 13)
As given line is perpendicular to PM, we have
10({tex}10\lambda{/tex} + 9) – 4({tex}-4\lambda{/tex} – 1) – 11({tex}-11\lambda{/tex} – 13) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M(1, 2, 3)
Let P’ ({tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}, {tex}\gamma{/tex}) be the image of the point P such that M is the mid-point of PP’.
{tex}\frac{\alpha+2}{2}{/tex} = 1; {tex}\frac{\beta-1}{2}{/tex} = 2; {tex} \frac{\gamma+5}{2}{/tex} = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0, {tex}\beta{/tex} = 5, {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 1
Hence, image of P(2, -1, 5) is P’(0, 5, 1)

33.

Find the inverse of the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}. Using the inverse A-1, solve the system of linear equations
x - y + 2z = 1; 2y - 3z = 1; 3x - 2y + 4z = 3.

Solution:

A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
|A| = 1(8 - 6) + 1(0 + 9) + 2(0 - 6) = -1 {tex}\neq{/tex} 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A is invertible.
adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 2 & 0 & -1 \\ -9 & -2 & 3 \\ -6 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 9 & 2 & -3 \\ 6 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
The given system of equation can be written as AX = B, when
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 2 & -3 \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = A-1 B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -2 & 0 & 1 \\ 9 & 2 & -3 \\ 6 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, y = 2, z = 1

35.(i)

If N denotes the set of all natural numbers and R be the relation on N {tex}\times{/tex} N defined by (a, b) R (c, d), if ad(b + c) = bc(a + d). Show that R is an equivalence relation.

Solution:

We have, a relation R on N {tex}\times{/tex} N defined by {tex}(a, b)R(c, d){/tex}, if {tex}ad(b + c) = bc(a + d){/tex}
Reﬂexive :

Let (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\times{/tex} N be any arbitrary element.
We have to show {tex}(a, b) R (a, b){/tex}, i.e. to show {tex}ab(b + a) = ba(a + b){/tex} which is trivially true as natural numbers are commutative under usual multiplication and addition.
Since, (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\times{/tex} N was arbitrary, therefore R is reﬂexive.
Symmetric:

Let (a, b), (c, d) {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\times{/tex} N such that{tex} (a, b) R (c, d){/tex}, i.e. {tex}ad(b + c) = bc(a + d){/tex} ... (i)
To show, (c, d) R (a, b), i.e. to show {tex}cb(d + a) = da(c + b){/tex}
From Eq.(i), we have {tex}ad(b + c) = bc(a + d)  da(c + b) = cb(d + a){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} natural numbers are commutative under usual addition and multiplication]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}cb(d + a) = da(c + b){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(c, d) R (a, b){/tex}
Thus, R is symmetric.
Transitive:
Let (a, b), (c, d) and (e, f) {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\times{/tex} N such that (a, b) R (c, d) and (c, d R (e, f)
Now, (a, b) R (c, d) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}ad(b + c) = bc(a + d)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { b + c } { b c } = \frac { a + d } { a d }{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { b } + \frac { 1 } { c } = \frac { 1 } { a } + \frac { 1 } { d }{/tex}.......(ii)
and (c, d) R (e, f) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}cf(d + e) = de(c + f)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d + e } { d e } = \frac { c + f } { c f }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { d } + \frac { 1 } { e } = \frac { 1 } { c } + \frac { 1 } { f }{/tex}......(iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { b } + \frac { 1 } { e } = \frac { 1 } { a } + \frac { 1 } { f }{/tex}{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { e + b } { b e } = \frac { f + a } { a f }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}af(e + b) = be(f + a){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}af(b+e)=be(a+f){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}(a, b) R (e, f){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}R is transitive
Thus, R is reﬂexive, symmetric and transitive, hence R is an equivalence relation.

OR

Let f: {tex}\mathbb{R}-\left\{-\frac{4}{3}\right\} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}{/tex} be a function defined as f(x) = {tex}\frac{4 \text x}{3 \text x+4}{/tex}. Show that f is a one-one function. Also, check whether f is an onto function or not.

Solution:

Let f: {tex}\mathbb{R}-\left\{-\frac{4}{3}\right\} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}{/tex} be a function defined as f(x) = {tex}\frac{4 \text x}{3 \text x+4}{/tex}.
For one-one (Injective):
Let f(x1) = f(x2) for some x1, x2 {tex}\in \text R-\left\{-\frac{4}{3}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{4 \text x_1}{3 \text x_1+4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 \text x_2}{3 \text x_2+4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x1x2 + 16x1 = 12x1x2 + 16x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x1 = 16x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is one-one function.
For onto (Surjective): Let y {tex}\in{/tex} R- {{tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}}, and for some x.
Let y = {tex}\frac{4 \text x}{3 \text x+4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3xy + 4y = 4x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(3y - 4) = -4y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}-\frac{4 \text y}{3 \text y-4}{/tex} or x = {tex}\frac{4 \text y}{4-3 \text y}{/tex}
x is real if {tex}y \neq \frac{4}{3}{/tex}.
So Rf = {tex}\text R-\left\{\frac{4}{3}\right\} \neq{/tex} Codomain(f)
So, f is not onto.

Case Study-1
36.

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A building contractor undertakes a job to construct 4 flats on a plot along with parking area. Due to strike the probability of many construction workers not being present for the job is 0.65. The probability that many are not present and still the work gets completed on time is 0.35. The probability that work will be completed on time when all workers are present is 0.80.
Let: E1: represent the event when many workers were not present for the job;
E2: represent the event when all workers were present; and
E: represent completing the construction work on time.

  1. What is the probability that all the workers are present for the job? (1)
  2. What is the probability that construction will be completed on time? (1)
  3. What is the probability that many workers are not present given that the construction work is completed on time? (2)
    OR
    What is the probability that all workers were present given that the construction job was completed on time? (2)
Solution:

  1. P(E2) = 1 - P(E1) = 1 – 0.65 = 0.35

  2. P(E) = P(E1){tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_1}\right){/tex} + P(E2){tex}\cdot \text P\left(\frac{\text E}{\text E_2}\right){/tex}
    = 0.65 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.35 + 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8
    = 0.35 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.45
    = 0.51

  3. {tex}\text P\left(\frac{\text E_1}{\text E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)}{\text P\left(\text E_1\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_1\right)+\text P\left(\text E_2\right) \cdot \text P\left(\frac {\text E}{\text E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.65 \times 0.35}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.45
    OR
    {tex}P\left(\frac{E_2}{E}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) \cdot P\left(\frac {E}{E}_2\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.35 \times 0.8}{0.51}{/tex} = 0.55

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Let f(x) be a real valued function. Then its

  • Left Hand Derivative (L.H.D.): Lf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a-\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{-\text h}{/tex}
  • Right Hand Derivative (R.H.D.): Rf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a+\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{\text h}{/tex}

Also, a function f(x) is said to be differentiable at x = a if its L.H.D. and R.H.D. at x = a exist and both are equal.
For the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} |\text x-3|, \text x \geq 1 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \text x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}

  1. What is R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1? (1)
  2. What is L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1? (1)
  3. Check if the function f(x) is differentiable at x = 1. (2)
    OR
    Find f'(2) and f'(-1). (2)
Solution:

  1. R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{|1+h-3|-|-2|}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{2-h-2}{h}{/tex} = -1

  2. L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[\frac{(1-h)^2}{4}-\frac{3(1-h)}{2}+\frac{13}{4}-2\right]}{-h}{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2-2 h+1-6+6 h+13-8}{-4 h}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2+4 h}{-4 h}\right]{/tex} = -1.

  3. Since L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1
    is same as R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1,
    f(x) is differentiable at x = 1.
    OR
    f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} \text x-3, \text x \geq 3 \\ 3-\text x, 1 \leq \text x<3 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \mathrm{x}<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
    [f'(x)]x=2 = 0 - 1 = -1
    [f'(x)]x=–1 = {tex}\frac{2(-1)}{4}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = -2

Solution:

  1. (a) 2x + y = 200
    (b) A(x) = xy = x(200 - x)

    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 50.
